topic: Kimi K2 vs Claude Opus vs GPT-5 Coding 2026: Moonshot's Model Benchmark

competitors.title: GPT-5.3-Codex vs Claude Opus 4.6 vs Kimi K2.5 (2026)
key_points: February 5, 2026 showdown: Claude Opus 4.6 launched, GPT-5.3-Codex dropped 20 minutes later with 77.3% Terminal-Bench vs Opus 4.6's 65.4%
SWE-Bench Verified near dead heat: Opus 4.6 at 80.8%, Kimi K2.5 at 76.8%
GDPval-AA knowledge work: Opus 4.6 leads by 144 Elo points over GPT-5.2 at 1606 Elo
Kimi K2.5 leads BrowseComp at 74.9% vs GPT-5.2 at 59.2%
Pricing: GPT-5.3-Codex $10/$30 per M tokens, Opus 4.6 $5/$25, Kimi K2.5 $0.60/$2.50 — 14.5× cost difference between Codex and Kimi
GPT-5.3-Codex instrumental in creating itself, 25% faster inference than predecessor
Opus 4.6 1M context window (beta) with 76% accuracy vs 18.5% for GPT-5.2 at same length
Enterprise LLM spending: $7M average in 2025 (180% YoY increase), projected $11.6M in 2026
Claude Sonnet 4.6: 79.6% SWE-bench at $3/$15 — near-Opus at 5× lower cost
Recommendation: Use Sonnet 4.6 daily driver, route to Opus for complex reasoning, Codex for terminal workflows, Kimi for cost-sensitive high-volume

competitors.title: Best Open-Source Coding Models in 2026: Benchmarks, VRAM, and Real Performance
key_points: February 2026 wave: MiniMax M2.5 (80.2% SWE-bench), GLM-5 (77.8%), Qwen3-Coder-Next (70.6% on 46GB)
Kimi K2.5: 76.8% SWE-bench, 85.0% LiveCodeBench (best in class), 1T/32B MoE, 256K context
Open-source gap compressed to single-digit percentages vs proprietary models
SWE-bench contamination caveat: OpenAI stopped reporting scores after confirming contamination
Kimi K2.5 best for visual coding: native multimodal (image-to-code, video-to-code, visual debugging)
Qwen3-Coder-Next runs on 46GB — only frontier-adjacent model for consumer hardware
DeepSeek V3.2: 73% SWE-bench, IOI 2025 gold medal, MIT license, 128K context
Mercury 2: 1,000 tokens/sec diffusion LM, 5× faster than autoregressive models, 67.3% LiveCodeBench
Open-source API pricing: 70-95% cheaper than proprietary ($0.22-$0.60 vs $5-$15 per M input)
WarpGrep adds +2.1-2.2% to SWE-Bench Pro for any base model with efficient search subagent

competitors.title: Kimi K2.5 in 2026: The Ultimate Guide to Open-Source Visual Agentic Intelligence
key_points: Agent Swarm: up to 100 sub-agents orchestrating 1,500+ parallel tool calls, 4.5× speed improvement via PARL
Four modes: Instant, Thinking, Agent, Agent Swarm (Beta)
Native multimodal: 15T mixed visual/text tokens, no separate vision encoder needed
Kimi Code CLI integrates with VS Code, Cursor, and Zed editors
API pricing: $0.60/M input, $3/M output (10× cheaper than Claude, 17× cheaper than Codex on input)
Agent Swarm vs traditional: up to 80% reduction in end-to-end runtime, 3-4.5× fewer critical steps
Visual debugging: screenshot → diagnosis → code fix pipeline without manual context switching
Modified MIT license for weights, HuggingFace available, vLLM/SGLang/TensorRT-LLM deployment
Hardware: ~2TB VRAM for full FP16, ~600GB INT4 quantized, significant infrastructure requirement

stats.fact: SWE-Bench Verified scores: Claude Opus 4.6 at 80.8%, MiniMax M2.5 at 80.2%, GLM-5 at 77.8%, Kimi K2.5 at 76.8%, DeepSeek V3.2 at 73%, Qwen3-Coder-Next at 70.6%
Terminal-Bench 2.0: GPT-5.3-Codex at 77.3%, Claude Opus 4.6 at 65.4% — nearly 12 percentage point gap
OSWorld-Verified: Claude Opus 4.6 at 72.7%, GPT-5.3-Codex at 64.7% — Opus leads by 8 points for GUI/computer use
GDPval-AA knowledge work: Claude Opus 4.6 at 1606 Elo, 144 points above GPT-5.2 — best for financial/legal/analysis coding
ARC-AGI-2 reasoning: Opus 4.6 at 68.8% (doubled from 37.6% in Opus 4.5), nearly 31 points in a single generation
Kimi K2.5 LiveCodeBench: 85.0% — best in class among open-source models, far ahead of Claude Opus 4.5 at 64.0%
Kimi K2.5 pricing at $0.60/$2.50 per M tokens is 10× cheaper than Claude and 17× cheaper than Codex on input tokens
Enterprise LLM spending: $7M average in 2025 (180% YoY increase), projected $11.6M in 2026; OpenAI share shrinking from 62% to 53%
Claude Opus 4.6 1M token context window (beta) maintains 76% accuracy vs GPT-5.2's 18.5% at same length (MRCR v2 benchmark)
Kimi K2.5 Humanity's Last Exam: 50.2%, beating Opus 4.6's 40.0% and matching GPT-5.2 Pro
February 2026 cluster: MiniMax M2.5 (80.2%), GLM-5 (77.8%), Qwen3-Coder-Next (70.6%) — gap between open and closed compressed to single-digit percentages
GPT-5.3-Codex was instrumental in creating itself: debugged own training runs, managed deployment infrastructure, wrote scaling scripts
Open-source models 70-95% cheaper: Qwen3-Coder $0.22/$1.00, MiniMax M2.5 $0.30/$1.20, Kimi K2.5 ~$0.40/$1.50 vs Claude Opus $15/$75 and GPT-5.2 ~$15/$60
February 5, 2026: Anthropic launched Opus 4.6, 20 minutes later OpenAI dropped GPT-5.3-Codex, while Kimi K2.5 shipped January 27

angles: The February 2026 Coding War: how three frontier models launched within 9 days and what it means for developers choosing a coding model
Open-source parity: Kimi K2.5 at 76.8% SWE-bench is within 4 points of Claude Opus 4.6 at 80.8% — but at 10× lower cost; when does open-source win?

Benchmark specialization matters: Codex dominates Terminal-Bench (77.3%), Opus leads OSWorld (72.7%) and knowledge work (GDPval-AA), Kimi excels at LiveCodeBench and visual coding — no single model wins everywhere
The agent architecture shift: Kimi's 100-sub-agent Swarm vs Claude's 16-agent Teams vs Codex's self-improving loop — how parallel orchestration changes coding productivity
Cost-to-performance ratio revolution: Kimi K2.5 at $55/month vs Codex at $800/month for 1,000 tasks; teams spending $50K+ annually should evaluate open-source
Context window wars: Opus 4.6's 1M token beta context with 76% accuracy vs Kimi's 256K and Codex's 400K — why context coherence matters more than size for large codebase work
SWE-bench contamination and real-world benchmarks: why SWE-Bench Pro, Terminal-Bench, and OSWorld tell different stories than SWE-bench Verified alone
keywords: claude opus 4.6 vs GPT-5 codex coding
kimi k2 vs claude opus coding benchmark
open source vs proprietary coding model 2026
outline: Introduction: The February 2026 Coding War Nobody Expected
Head-to-Head Benchmarks: SWE-bench, Terminal-Bench, OSWorld, LiveCodeBench, GDPval-AA
GPT-5.3-Codex: The Terminal Terminator — 77.3% Terminal-Bench, 25% faster inference, self-debugging
Claude Opus 4.6: The Enterprise Brain — 80.8% SWE-bench, 1M context, Agent Teams, OSWorld leader
Kimi K2.5: The Open-Source Wildcard — 76.8% SWE-bench, 85% LiveCodeBench, 10× cheaper, Agent Swarm
The Open-Source Wave: MiniMax M2.5, GLM-5, Qwen3-Coder-Next, DeepSeek V3.2
Pricing Deep Dive: $55/month vs $500/month vs $800/month — where your money goes
Agent Architecture Comparison: Agent Swarm (100 agents) vs Agent Teams (16 agents) vs Codex self-loop
Context Window and Long-Context Coherence: Opus 1M vs Kimi 256K vs Codex 400K
When to Use Which Model: Decision Framework by Use Case
Visual Coding and Multimodal Capabilities: Kimi's native multimodal advantage
Enterprise Considerations: Cost at scale, vendor lock-in, ecosystem maturity, compliance
The Bigger Picture: What Open-Source Parity Means for the AI Coding Market
Claude Sonnet 4.6: The Dark Horse at $3/$15 per M tokens
SWE-bench Contamination: Why SWE-Bench Pro Matters More Than Verified
Conclusion and Recommendations: Daily Driver → Complex Reasoning → Terminal Workflows → Cost-Sensitive
